---
title: "Discussion"
author: "Mikkel E. Steentoft"
suppress-bibliography: true
---

In this study, we used k-means clustering to identify three subgroups with distinct responses on glucose, insulin, GLP-1, GIP and glucagon derived from the three point oral glucose tolerance test. Importantly, the clustering revealed phenotypes that differed not only in glycemic status, but also in distinct incretin and glucagon dynamics, suggesting that postprandial regulation is heterogeneous beyond glucose alone.

### Hormonal responses during the OGTT

Cluster 3 was the most metabolically compromised and adipose subgroup, with insulin resistance and compensatory hyperinsulinemia, dysglycemia, highest VAT, BMI and body fat percentage. This group was also the oldest and had the highest proportion of smokers and individuals with type 2 diabetes, IFG+IGT and IGT. Cluster 3 had elevated fasting insulin levels (53.6 pmol/L) and a 2-hour insulin level of 387.2 pmol/L, alongside the highest 2hPG of 9.1 mmol/L. This pattern is consistent with insulin resistance along with compensatory hyperinsulinemia, suggesting some degree of preserved beta-cell function, but that the hypersecretion of insulin is not sufficient to compensate entirely for the insulin resistance [@defronzo2004]. Glucagon regulation was also impaired in cluster 3, with an early (0-30 minutes) increase in glucagon, despite an adequate late glucagon suppression. Low or negative glucagon suppression results in continued hepatic glucose output in the early postprandial period, contributing to the elevated glucose levels. This is consistent with prior evidence linking impaired early glucagon suppression to insulin resistance and progression to type 2 diabetes [@færch2016; @wagner2017; @knop2007]. Cluster 3 had almost similar overall glucagon suppression to cluster 1 (45.4% vs 47%), despite having very different early and late patterns. This may suggest impaired early-phase inhibition of glucagon secretion, but also that the compensatory insulin secretion observed by 120 minutes eventually results in adequate late glucagon suppression. This may reflect that glucagon suppression in cluster 3 is achieved through a delayed and compensatory mechanism, rather than the efficient and early suppression in cluster 1 [@gromada2007]. This complexity is further illustrated by the findings in cluster 2. Despite cluster 2 having the highest insulin sensitivity, this group had lower glucagon suppression at all intervals compared to cluster 1, but this did not result in elevated glucose levels. The finding of non-suppressed glucagon<sub>120</sub> among insulin-sensitive individuals is in line with previous findings [@færch2016; @wagner2017], which suggests that cluster 2's low glucagon suppression<sub>120</sub> may reflect a metabolically healthier phenotype rather than dysregulation. Interestingly, cluster 1 and 2 had relatively similar insulin sensitivity and proportions of individuals with NGT, but very different incretin secretions, with cluster 1 having the lowest incretin secretion across all clusters. The preserved glucagon suppression in cluster 1, despite low incretin levels, suggests that their insulin secretion was sufficient to inhibit glucagon secretion under current conditions [@gromada2007]. While incretins are typically important regulators of both insulin and glucagon secretion, these mechanisms are known to be glucose-dependent [@christensen2011], indicating that in insulin-sensitive individuals, adequate glucagon suppression can be maintained even when incretin secretion is reduced. However, the observation of lower incretin secretion in cluster 1 also raises the question of whether this reflects a subgroup at higher risk of developing type 2 diabetes, as lower levels of GLP-1 previously have been indicated to already be present in individuals with prediabetes within this cohort [@færch2015]. Conversely, cluster 3 exhibits higher incretin secretion than cluster 1, indicating that the interplay between impaired glucose metabolism, obesity and incretin secretion is complex. This is underscored by several studies, showing both decreased [@laakso2008; @muscelli2006; @toft-nielsen2001], normal [@vollmer2008; @ahren1997] and increased [@chong2022; @færch2008] incretin secretion among individuals with prediabetes and type 2 diabetes compared to normoglycemic individuals. The higher GLP-1 secretion in cluster 3 may reflect a compensatory mechanism to maintain sufficient insulin secretion as suggested previously [@færch2008]. The elevated GIP may also partly reflect adiposity-related GIP secretion, consistent with prior evidence linking obesity to higher fasting and postprandial GIP levels [@vilsbøll2003]. In summary, these findings highlight that the hormonal responses of dysglycemia differ between subgroups, providing nuance to the endocrine heterogeneity in prediabetes and diabetes.

### Associations with body fat distribution

Beyond the dynamic hormonal responses, the three clusters differed in their associations with body fat distribution. The insulin-resistant profile of cluster 3 was associated with higher VAT, SAT, VAT/SAT ratio and body fat percentage, in line with previous findings from the ADDITION-PRO cohort [@philipsen2015], in which higher measures of obesity was associated with lower insulin sensitivity, higher FPG and 2hPG. Furthermore, excess VAT has been associated with all categories of impaired glucose metabolism, including iIFG, iIGT, IFG+IGT and newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes [@borel2015]. Consistent with this, VAT and the VAT/SAT ratio have been shown to increase progressively from NGT through prediabetes to type 2 diabetes [@maskarinec2022]. Interestingly, cluster 1 and 2 were markedly different in SAT and VAT/SAT ratio, while cluster 2 had slightly higher VAT, but lower body fat percentage than cluster 1. Higher SAT has previously been suggested to be a protective metabolic trait, as it was found to be inversely associated with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes, with a more pronounced association in women [@borel2015]. Another study found that VAT, but not SAT was higher in individuals with type 2 diabetes compared to healthy individuals, regardless of BMI [@adhikary2023]. This suggests that fat distribution in cluster 1 is more favorable, with a relatively greater proportion stored subcutaneously, which may partially compensate for their higher absolute adiposity compared to cluster 2.

### Sex differences

Sex-stratified clustering was assessed through a sensitivity analysis, which produced similar phenotypes in both men and women as the non-stratified analysis. In particular, women had higher total GLP-1 and GIP but lower glucagon suppression across all clusters. Higher overall GLP-1 secretion in women is consistent with the finding of Faerch et al. in the same cohort [@færch2015]. Interaction terms between cluster membership and sex were non-significant in all models, with the largest difference in VAT and SAT for cluster 1 and 3 and VAT/SAT ratio for cluster 1. Fat distribution is known to differ between men and women [@bredella2017], and in particular, VAT has been shown to have stronger association to cardiometabolic risk in women [@schorr2018]. Overall, consistency between non-stratified and stratified clustering solutions support the robustness of the primary analysis, consistent with the findings in a previous clustering study of prediabetes [@stroebel2025]. Interestingly, Stroebel et al. also found that their subcutaneous-adipose group, which had 84% women and the highest proportion of SAT, showed the lowest risk for developing type 2 diabetes despite high BMI and waist circumference. This suggests that sex-differences in fat distribution and their metabolic implications may be clinically relevant, even though we did not find significant differences in this study.

### Metabolic heterogeneity in prediabetes

The findings of distinct subgroups in this study align with previous evidence assessing heterogeneity in prediabetes [@stroebel2025; @onthoni2025; @wagner2021]. However, to our knowledge, this is the first study including the incretin and glucagon dimension in a cluster analysis among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes. This study found subgroups corresponding to the ones identified in other studies using glycemic and obesity measures, with cluster 3 being the overweight, insulin-resistant group with a high proportion of people with type 2 diabetes, and cluster 1 and 2 being the more healthy, insulin-sensitive subgroups with mostly normoglycemic individuals. However, these studies quantified the risk of developing type 2 diabetes using follow-up data [@stroebel2025; @wagner2021], while the present study utilized a cross-sectional design, unable to assess similar temporal associations. Wagner et al. identified six clusters, of which three high-risk clusters characterized by visceral adiposity and insulin resistance broadly corresponded to cluster 3 in our study, while their three low-risk clusters correspond to clusters 1 and 2 [@wagner2021]. Interestingly, Stroebel et al. found that their cluster C, with higher SAT relative to VAT, but higher BMI and waist circumference was protected from incident T2D compared to cluster A with lower BMI and waist circumference, but higher VAT compared to SAT [@stroebel2025]. This aligns with our findings, with cluster 1 having a lower VAT/SAT ratio despite having overall higher fat percentage and BMI compared to cluster 2. While differences in variable selection limits direct comparisons with prior studies, the broad pattern one high-risk insulin resistant cluster and two lower-risk metabolically healthier clusters is consistent with previous findings. However, the inclusion of incretin and glucagon responses seems to reveal heterogeneity that glycemic and obesity measures did not capture. Most notably, cluster 3 exhibits a higher incretin secretion than cluster 1, despite being metabolically compromised in every other measure of both glucose metabolism and obesity. Furthermore, cluster 2 has the lowest overall glucagon suppression but highest incretin secretion, and these traits seem to be adequate to maintain normal insulin sensitivity. Thus, the main difference between cluster 1 and 2 seems to be reflected in their different incretin secretion and glucagon suppression.

<!-- tilføj noget om vigtigheden af inkretiner/tarmens rolle??-->

While the identification of metabolic subgroups reflects a broader shift in diabetes [@ahlqvist2018; @zou2019; @bej2022] and prediabetes research [@wagner2021; @stroebel2025; @onthoni2025], the clinical use of data-driven phenotyping approaches has been questioned. Dennis et al. found that simple, continuous features, such as age at diagnosis, predicted glycemic progression as well as more complex, clustering-based approaches [@dennis2019]. In addition, the use of hard clustering methods like k-means clustering raises the question of how well each individual fit the subgroup they are assigned to. Thus, clustering analysis should be accompanied by a measure of classification uncertainty [@mori2025]. In this study, we used the silhouette width to quantify the overall clustering quality in line with previous studies [@wagner2021; @onthoni2025; @stroebel2025]. The challenges of using more complex, data-driven phenotyping approaches in type 2 diabetes research are underscored by an international consensus report by the Precision Medicine in Diabetes Initiative, stating that the most promising approaches to precision medicine in type 2 diabetes are to use simple, continuous features for predicting treatment outcomes [@tobias2023]. Consequently, rather than aiming to predict outcomes, clustering methods like k-means clustering could be used as a tool to explore the interplay between potential pathophysiological traits that simple glycemic measures cannot reveal.

### Strengths and limitations

The strengths of this study are the relatively large cohort of individuals with NGT, prediabetes and type 2 diabetes, the sensitive and specific methods of analyzing plasma for glucagon, GLP-1 and GIP through the OGTT [@holst1983; @orskov1994; @lindgren2011; @toft-nielsen2001] and detailed measures of fat distribution through ultrasound.

However, several limitations should be considered. First, the cross-sectional design implies that the findings cannot be used to define clear associations of cause and effect between cluster characteristics and patterns of fat distribution. While this is an exploratory study of the interplay between glucose, insulin, GLP-1, GIP and glucagon and how this relates to fat distribution, it is not critical that temporal associations cannot be determined. Second, we did not perform a fully sex-stratified analysis with clustering and regression models for men and women separately, which may have limited our ability to identify sex-specific phenotypes. In addition, because male sex contributed to the risk score used to identify the high-risk population in the ADDITION-DK study [@glümer2004], women included in the cohort may have had a higher burden of other risk factors. This may have reduced the comparability between men and women and affected the sex differences. Third, there are limitations in the use of k-means clustering to identify phenotypes. The center for each cluster is calculated as a mean of all the observations in the cluster, which implies that extreme values can shift the center of the cluster disproportional to the observations [@khan2024]. Moreover, the average silhouette width indicates that some individuals fit their cluster better than others, which may reflect that some phenotypes overlap rather than fitting into one single cluster [@mori2025]. Fourth, participants excluded from the clustering analysis tended to have higher BMI, visceral fat and HbA1c compared to those included. Thus, the included participants may reflect a healthier sample of individuals at high risk of diabetes, which may affect both the generalisability of the identified clusters and the strength of the observed associations with fat distribution. Finally, with this study being cross-sectional and more exploratory, the question of whether the differences in incretins and glucagon between these subgroups are clinically relevant needs to be raised. Further, prospective studies should establish the temporal associations between traditional glycemic traits, incretins, glucagon and measures of fat distribution and how these characteristics relate to hard outcomes such as progression to type 2 diabetes or cardiovascular complications.

### Conclusion

In summary, we identified three distinct subgroups among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes, characterized by differences in incretin secretion, glucagon regulation and body fat distribution. Notably, subgroups with similar glycemic status differed in incretin secretion and glucagon regulation. This suggests that heterogeneity in hormonal responses represents a biologically meaningful dimension of prediabetes that standard glycemic classification cannot capture. Whether these differences relate to different risk trajectories of developing type 2 diabetes and related complications or reflect different responses to prevention and treatment strategies need to be established in prospective studies.
